title: Setting up a piped water supply for livestock
subtitle: Running a permanent or semi-permanent water line to livestock troughs from a mains or tank supply.
excerpt: Carrying water to livestock in buckets is fine for a handful of animals for a short period. For any smallholding running more than a few animals through the year, a piped supply to a trough in each paddock removes one of the most labour-intensive and easily missed daily tasks. This covers the options for supply, pipe specification, trough choice, and frost protection.
sourceNotes:
DEFRA, Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Livestock: Cattle (2003). https://www.gov.uk
Water Regulations Advisory Scheme (WRAS), backflow prevention guidance. https://www.wras.co.uk

glossaryTerms.definition: A float-controlled valve inside the trough that opens when the water level drops and closes when the level reaches the set point. The standard method for maintaining a constant water level in livestock troughs without manual filling. Ball valves require periodic cleaning to prevent calcium buildup or debris fouling the seat.

A device or arrangement that prevents water that has entered a trough from flowing back into the mains supply. Required by Water Supply (Water Fittings) Regulations 1999 wherever a pipe connects to the mains and terminates below the maximum water level in a vessel. A simple air-gap (the pipe end terminating above the maximum water level) satisfies the regulation for most livestock trough installations.

content.text: A trough filled automatically by a

needs daily inspection but not daily filling. Check: the water level is correct, the ball valve is moving freely, the trough is not fouled with dung, and the pipe is not leaking. This takes 30 seconds per trough per day versus several minutes per animal per bucket.

A mains connection to the nearest point in a field is the most reliable supply. Where mains pressure is low or the distance is large, an intermediate header tank on a stand raises the hydrostatic pressure to the trough. For fields with no mains access, a gravity-feed system from an IBC tank on higher ground works well if the height difference is at least 3 metres; below that, flow to a ball valve is too slow for multiple animals.

Use blue MDPE (medium density polyethylene) pipe for any supply connected to the mains: this is the UK standard for potable water below ground. 20 mm diameter is adequate for a single trough; 25 mm for two or more troughs on the same run. Bury pipe to at least 750 mm depth to prevent frost damage. Where pipe must cross the surface (a gate threshold, for example), lag it with pipe insulation and a protective conduit.
The Water Supply (Water Fittings) Regulations 1999 require

wherever a mains supply terminates in a vessel that an animal can contaminate. The simplest compliant solution is to terminate the inlet pipe at least 25 mm above the overflow point of the trough: this air-gap means the pipe can never be submerged in contaminated water regardless of what the animals do to the trough.

Livestock deprived of water in cold weather deteriorate quickly. In exposed fields, fit a frost-protection cover (a hinged lid or insulated jacket) over the trough and ball valve. For severe frost risk, a small electric trough heater element (25 W) on a thermostat maintains open water down to minus 15 degrees C. Drain any above-ground sections of pipe overnight if a hard frost is forecast: a burst pipe in a field that drains undetected overnight loses the water supply for the day at minimum.
